summary:
The Southern and Northern Dynasties period (420–589 AD) was an era of prolonged political division in China following the collapse of the Jin dynasty, during which the north was dominated by a succession of dynasties founded by non-Han nomadic peoples (the Northern dynasties, most significantly the Northern Wei ruled by the Tuoba Xianbei) while the south saw a series of Chinese dynasties centered on Jiankang (Nanjing) maintaining Han cultural traditions. The period is notable for the deep interpenetration of Chinese and nomadic cultures in the north, and for the explosive spread of Buddhism throughout China.

The Northern Wei (386–534 AD) is perhaps the most significant northern dynasty — under Emperor Xiaowen (r. 471–499 AD), the court undertook radical Sinicization policies, moving the capital from Pingcheng to Luoyang, mandating Chinese dress and language at court, and adopting Chinese surnames for the Xianbei elite. The Yungang and Longmen cave temple complexes, carved during this period, represent some of the greatest Buddhist art in Chinese history. In the south, aristocratic culture, landscape poetry, and calligraphy flourished at the courts of the Song, Qi, Liang, and Chen dynasties. The division finally ended when the Sui dynasty reunified China in 589, but the cultural, ethnic, and religious transformations of this period fundamentally shaped the Tang dynasty civilization that followed.
